# Abstract
We estimate the impact of transit investments on urban employment. Panel data from 50 cities are analyzed.

# Introduction
Infrastructure spending can reshape labor markets. Evidence on transit effects remains mixed.

# Methods
We use a difference in differences design with city fixed effects. Controls include income, housing supply, and population.

# Results
Transit expansion is associated with a 1.8 percent employment increase over five years. Effects are stronger in mid sized cities.

Figure 1. Employment trends before and after transit expansion.

# Discussion
Results suggest complementary housing policies may amplify benefits. Limitations include unobserved local shocks.

Table 1. Regression coefficients for baseline and extended models.

# Conclusion
Transit investment supports modest employment growth in urban areas. Policy design should consider equity outcomes.

# References
[1] O. Economist. Transit policy and labor markets. 2013.
[2] P. Economist. Infrastructure impacts meta analysis. 2020.
